user_prompt: question, is VBUS a GPIO? point out where it is initialized as an input
agent_reply: No, `VBUS` here is not an ESP32 GPIO. It’s a power/PMIC signal being read over I2C through M5Unified.
For Core2 / AXP192, VBUS is read from PMIC registers:
The nearest thing to “initialized as input” is not a GPIO input setup. It’s PMIC ADC enable:
- AXP192: `0x82, 0xFF // reg82h ADC all on` in [Power_Class.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/.pio/libdeps/thefly_bringuptest/M5Unified/src/utility/Power_Class.cpp:464)
- AXP2101 Core2 v1.1: `0x30, 0x0F // ADC enabled` in [Power_Class.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/.pio/libdeps/thefly_bringuptest/M5Unified/src/utility/Power_Class.cpp:534)
So: no `pinMode(VBUS, INPUT)` exists because VBUS is not exposed to the ESP32 as a normal GPIO in this path. It’s PMIC telemetry.

user_prompt: if `gui->showView(FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN)` happens after `bt_host_list->loadFromMicroSd()`, why does it seem like it took time for the phone and laptop items to load?
agent_reply: Yes, your instinct is right, but there’s a second thing making it look delayed.

`MainScreenView` is constructed early as a static in [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:32), before `setup()` creates and loads `bt_host_list`. Its constructor calls `syncBluetoothHostButtonFades()`, and at that point `bt_host_list == nullptr`, so phone/laptop start faded.
But the bigger issue is this: [SplashView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/SplashView.cpp:31) automatically calls:
after the splash delay. That happens inside `show_splash()`, which currently runs before:
So the main view is actually shown once before the Bluetooth JSON is loaded.

`FlyGui::showView()` sees that main is already the current view and returns immediately without `onLoad()` or forced redraw. Then a later normal poll/redraw finally runs `MainScreenView::redraw()`, calls `syncBluetoothHostButtonFades()`, and the icons catch up.

So the real cause is not just early construction. It’s that `SplashView` hands off to main too early, and the later explicit `showView(MAIN)` becomes a no-op. Best fix later: remove the splash view’s automatic transition and let `setup()` be the only place that transitions to main after all boot data is loaded.

agent_reply: I compared `developer-docs/ui-design.md` against the current FlyGui/main flow. - `BTConnect` and `BTPair` are both represented by `ConnWaitingView` modes, not separate view classes: [all_init.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:180>).

- Cloud upload completion always reboots after dismissing the completion/failure dialog, matching the diagram’s success path but also doing it for failure completion: [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:877>).

- `Cloud` as a user-visible menu is not reachable from the current flow, despite `ScrollView::populateCloud()` existing.
- `CloudEnroll` and `CloudEnrollConfirmation` are not implemented as GUI flows.

- `WiFiAP --> password reset/memory reset --> Boot` is therefore not a FlyGui transition.
- `BTConnect --> cancel --> Main` is slightly different: cancel returns to the remembered previous view, often the Bluetooth scroll, not always Main.
- `BTErrorDialog --> Main` is often `BTErrorDialog --> Scroll`, because connection errors return to the Bluetooth list.
- “Change volume during actual calls” does not appear implemented in the recording UI. There is mic/speaker mode and speaker mute behavior, but not volume adjustment.
- “Mute or unmute mic” is only partially aligned; the UI toggles audio mode and speaker mute, not a clear mic mute/unmute control.

Incoming Bluetooth connection to Recording is substantially implemented through pending flags and `on_bluetooth_state_changed`/recording handling. It applies broadly, with special suppression during pairing confirmation and with Wi-Fi AP activation shutting Bluetooth down, which matches the “except when Wi-Fi radio owns the radio” intent.

user_prompt: I am looking at my serial terminal, I see "NVS initialized" as the last message, I have Verbose logging configured, why am I not seeing more messages? did something shutdown the serial port?
agent_reply: No, I don’t see anything shutting down the serial port.
What’s happening is likely logging-level mismatch:
- `NVS initialized` is a raw `Serial.printf()` in [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:100), so it appears.
- The very next normal boot message is `ESP_LOGI(...)` in [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:326), not `Serial.printf`.

- Your app defines verbose flags in `platformio.ini`, but the prebuilt ESP-IDF sdkconfig is still capped at `CONFIG_LOG_DEFAULT_LEVEL_ERROR` and `CONFIG_LOG_MAXIMUM_LEVEL=1`. That means `ESP_LOGI`, `ESP_LOGD`, etc. won’t show. Error logs should still show.
I also checked M5Unified: it only calls `Serial.begin()` if `cfg.serial_baudrate` is nonzero, and our `init_m5()` does not set that. No `Serial.end()` in our code.

So the port is probably alive; normal ESP-IDF info/debug logs are just being suppressed. Best quick diagnostic is to add a few temporary `Serial.printf()` breadcrumbs after `check_reset_flag()`, `init_m5()`, `init_gui()`, and `BattTracker::shutdownIfNeeded()` to see where boot is actually reaching.
